This welcome news for wildlife wasn’t just the result of good luck. For more than twenty years, scientists and volunteers across the country have worked to help the bald eagle.

content.passage: As the school bus rumbled toward home, Marisa thought about the reasons she didn’t want to go to Penmark School. First of all, she’d had to leave all her friends in California to come to Maine. Second, her family needed to move two weeks after the school year started so that Mom could start her new job at the medical center. And third? Well, Marisa couldn’t think of a third, but she figured those two were enough for her first day. Marisa stared out at the fields rolling by. She sighed and reached into her backpack. At least she had a good mystery to read. But she hadn’t even read a whole sentence from her book before a voice next to her made her jump. “Hey, I’ve read that one. It’s great.” Marisa turned to see a girl in a fuzzy purple sweater. “I’m Shelly,” said the girl, and she grinned so hard her gums showed. Marisa felt herself smiling back. “I’m Marisa,” she said. “We have the same homeroom, right?” Shelly nodded. Then she pointed to the book Marisa held. “I have to tell you—,” she began. “No!” Marisa covered her ears with her hands. “Don’t tell me.”
content.sentence: As Marissa rides the bus home, she first [dropdown]. Then, Marissa [dropdown]. At school the next day, Marissa [dropdown].

items.instructions: Read the paragraph. Select the two phrases that help you understand the meaning of “don’t rush.”

content.passage: Finally, you’re ready to take a hike. What will you see? Whether you hike in the forest, the desert, or near a swamp, you’re sure to see many wonders of nature. As you go on your first hike, don’t rush. Walk at a leisurely pace. You’ll soon fall into a comfortable stride. This first hike might be just to observe. Take in the sights, sounds, and smells of the area. What do you see? At each place you hike, you will see different things. Watch for bird nests, snake skins, feathers, nursery logs, mushrooms, fish, animal tracks. Soon you’ll realize that much of the fun of hiking is in studying nature and much of the fun of studying nature is in the detective work. Who made these tracks? What kind of bird dropped this feather? Every good detective takes notes. How about making a hiking journal for your trek? You can include notes on the date of the hike, weather conditions, where you are going, and a diagram of the landscape. Sketch the animal tracks you see along the creek, add a feather you found, draw the tadpole you saw in the big puddle. Maybe later you can find out how long it will be before the tadpole becomes a frog. You can also add photographs from your hike—like the chipmunk that chattered at you while you ate lunch, and your family members when they jumped into the creek.
content.sentence: As you go on your first hike, don’t rush. Walk at a leisurely pace. You’ll soon fall into a comfortable stride. This first hike might be just to observe. Take in the sights, sounds, and smells of the area. What do you see? At each place you hike, you will see different things.

items.instructions: Which statement best describes the difference in the point of view of “The Eagles Are Back!” and “Bald eagles make a comeback in Chicago”?
items.practiceHint: (Practice Hint: Select to Enlarge to make the answer options larger.)

content.passage: The Eagles Are Back!
choices: A. “The Eagles Are Back!” is written from the point of view of a scientist interested in explaining the problems faced by bald eagles, while “Bald eagles make a comeback in Chicago” is written from the point of view of a biologist and a politician studying the effects of pesticides on bald eagles.

B. “The Eagles Are Back!” is written from the point of view of a student researching bald eagles, while “Bald eagles make a comeback in Chicago” is written from the point of view of a park ranger who discusses how parks have brought back bald eagles.

C. “The Eagles Are Back!” is written from the point of view of an individual providing a general history of bald eagles throughout the country, while “Bald eagles make a comeback in Chicago” is written from the point of view of a reporter who discusses sightings of bald eagles in a particular area of the country.

D. “The Eagles Are Back!” is written from the point of view of a resident who has sighted bald eagles, while “Bald eagles make a comeback in Chicago” is written from the point of view of a historian who describes where bald eagles used to live.

content.passage: Mystery Club by Maurissa Guibord As the school bus rumbled toward home, Marisa thought about the reasons she didn’t want to go to Penmark School. First of all, she’d had to leave all her friends in California to come to Maine. Second, her family needed to move two weeks after the school year started so that Mom could start her new job at the medical center. And third? Well, Marisa couldn’t think of a third, but she figured those two were enough for her first day. Marisa stared out at the fields rolling by. She sighed and reached into her backpack. At least she had a good mystery to read. But she hadn’t even read a whole sentence from her book before a voice next to her made her jump. “Hey, I’ve read that one. It’s great.” Marisa turned to see a girl in a fuzzy purple sweater. “I’m Shelly,” said the girl, and she grinned so hard her gums showed. Marisa felt herself smiling back. “I’m Marisa,” she said. “We have the same homeroom, right?” Shelly nodded. Then she pointed to the book Marisa held. “I have to tell you—,” she began. “No!” Marisa covered her ears with her hands. “Don’t tell me how it ends!”

content.passage: Read the following passage about taking a hike. Trekking the Trail by Linda White Finally, you’re ready to take a hike. What will you see? Whether you hike in the forest, the desert, or near a swamp, you’re sure to see many wonders of nature. As you go on your first hike, don’t rush. Walk at a leisurely pace. You’ll soon fall into a comfortable stride. This first hike might be just to observe. Take in the sights, sounds, and smells of the area. What do you see? At each place you hike, you will see different things. Watch for bird nests, snake skins, feathers, nursery logs, mushrooms, fish, animal tracks. Soon you’ll realize that much of the fun of hiking is in studying nature and much of the fun of studying nature is in the detective work. Who made these tracks? What kind of bird dropped this feather? Every good detective takes notes. How about making a hiking journal for your trek? You can include notes on the date of the hike, weather conditions, where you are going, and a diagram of the landscape. Sketch the animal tracks you see along the creek, add a feather you found, draw the tadpole you saw in the big puddle. Maybe later you can find out how long it will be before the tadpole becomes a frog. You can also add photographs from your hike—like the chipmunk that chattered at you along the trail, and your family members when they reached the top of the hill.
content.sentence: As you go on your first hike, don’t rush. Walk at a leisurely pace. You’ll soon fall into a comfortable stride. This first hike might be just to observe. Take in the sights, sounds, and smells of the area. What do you see? At each place you hike, you will see different things.

content.passage: Nail Soup a folktale from Sweden There was once a man who went plodding his way through a forest. The distance between the houses was so great that he had little hope of finding a shelter before the night set in. But all of a sudden he saw some lights between the trees. He then discovered a cottage, where there was a fire burning on the hearth. How nice it would be to roast one’s self before that fire, and to get a bite of something, he thought, and so he dragged himself towards the cottage. Just then a woman came toward him. “Good evening, and well met!” said the man. “Good evening,” said the woman. “Where do you come from?” “South of the sun and east of the moon,” said the man, “and now I am on the way home again, for I have been all over the world with the exception of this parish,” he said. “You must be a great traveler then,” said the woman. “What may be your business here?”
sources: A hungry and tired man was wandering through the forest.
The man kindly suggested a way to provide them both with a meal.
The man was determined, so woman finally allowed him to sleep on the floor.
The woman was in a good mood after learning something useful from the man.
Some ingredients were provided and added to the pot after each discussion.
She brought out her food from her cupboard and set the table fit for a king.
He encountered a woman who was greedy and always complaining.
The man left after the woman provided him with breakfast, money, and thanks.

items.practiceHint: Select to Enlarge to make the answer options larger.

content.passage: Editorial: Teens are going to extremes with texting The Jersey Journal The national obsession with instantaneous communication is taking a toll on teens so severe that some experts are calling it a crisis. It’s not the phenomenon of cell-phoning or messaging while driving—both are illegal in New Jersey—but all-night texting that is leaving too many teens too tired for school. One 14-year-old New Jerseyan featured in a recent Star-Ledger story receives up to 10,000—10,000!—text messages a month. To accomplish that astounding yet not unusual feat, daily activities like bathing and sleeping are interrupted, thumbs pumping, to read and respond to an avalanche of messages. Doctors are beginning to recognize such obsession as addiction that is robbing children of sleep at the very time in their lives when they need it the most.

items.instructions: Move two pieces of evidence from the passage that best support the claim that many teens who text are too tired for school.
items.practiceHint: (Practice Hint: Move your answers into the empty box. To change your answers, select the Clear All button.)

content.passage: Editorial: Teens are going to extremes with texting The Jersey Journal The national obsession with instantaneous communication is taking a toll on teens so severe that some experts are calling it a crisis. It’s not the phenomenon of cell-phoning or messaging while driving—both are illegal in New Jersey—but all-night texting that is leaving too many teens too tired for school. One 14-year-old New Jerseyan featured in a recent Star-Ledger story receives up to 10,000—10,000!—text messages a month. To accomplish that astounding yet not unusual feat, daily activities like bathing and sleeping are interrupted, thumbs pumping, to read and respond to an avalanche of messages. Doctors are beginning to recognize such obsession as addiction that is robbing children of sleep at the very time in their lives when they need it the most. A well-publicized study recently found that early high school start times deprive adolescents of sleep and force students to perform academically in the early morning, a time of day when they are at their worst. Many teens are making a tough situation worse by shortchanging themselves of the time they do have to sleep. The inevitable results are poor performance, a sort of sleepwalking through classes and the day in a fog.
sources: "To accomplish that astounding yet not unusual feat, daily activities like bathing and sleeping are interrupted..."
"...early high school start times deprive adolescents of sleep and force students to perform academically in the early morning, a time of day when they are at their worst..."
"The inevitable results are poor performance, a sort of sleepwalking through classes and the day in a fog..."
"...these kids are among the first to have this amazing, and lengthy, technology grafted at their fingertips; it’s not likely they will limit it for themselves..."
"A great many adults are also addicted to devices of the new technology..."

One 14-year-old New Jerseyan featured in a recent Star-Ledger story receives up to 10,000—10,000!—text messages a month. To accomplish that astounding yet not unusual feat, daily activities like bathing and sleeping are interrupted, thumbs pumping, to read and respond to an avalanche of messages. Doctors are beginning to recognize such obsession as addiction that is robbing children of sleep at the very time in their lives when they need it the most. A well-publicized study recently found that early high school start times deprive adolescents of sleep and force students to perform academically in the early morning, a time of day when they are at their worst. Many teens are making a tough situation worse by shortchanging themselves of the time they do have to sleep. The inevitable results are poor performance, a sort of sleepwalking through classes and the day in a fog.
sources: "To accomplish that astounding yet not unusual feat, daily activities like bathing and sleeping are interrupted..."
"...early high school start times deprive adolescents of sleep and force students to perform academically in the early morning, a time of day when they are at their worst."
"The inevitable results are poor performance, a sort of sleepwalking through classes and the day in a fog."
"...teens who are among the first to have this amazing, and tempting, technology that is their fingertips; it’s not likely they will text less for themselves."
"A great many adults are also addicted to devices of the new technology."

content.paragraph: (1) Collecting rocks has been one of my hobbies since I was very young. (2) Of the three types of rock, my favorite is igneous rock, which is formed as lava cools and changes from a liquid to a solid. (3) Unlike sedimentary rock, which forms when a layer of small fragments, or sediment, are packed together by pressure.(4) Sedimentary rocks often have interesting layers, or bands, that form patterns throughout the rock.(5) Still, I prefer the glossy quality that many igneous rocks have.

items.instructions: Select the check marks in the table to show whether an event is in “Imagine That,” “Meet My Friends,” or both.
items.practiceHint: Use the Master Pointer tool to select the boxes you want to place check marks in. To erase a check mark once it is placed, click on it.

content.passage: Imagine That Eagerly, Jack helped his big sister set up their tent in the campground. It was Jack’s first camping adventure. Once the tent was proudly standing, they headed to the playground. Then they walked through the woods to the lake. Jack and his sister waded in the water and sailed some sticks. Finally, they returned to their tent. Everyone was tired. Dad got some sandwiches, and they ate from their car. “Why don’t we put our food in the tent?” asked Jack. “It might attract animals,” Dad said. They sat on a blanket to eat.
sources: Someone must overcome a fear.
Someone tries to hide when afraid.
A brother and sister help each other.
A brother and sister go on an outdoor adventure.
A brother and sister spend time with some friends.

items.instructions: Read the paragraphs from “The Legend of the Black Fish.” Then, choose two sentences that show a central message of the passage.
items.practiceHint: Use the Masking tool to cover some of the information on the screen.

content.passage: Long ago, a young man named Natsilane lived on the Pacific Northwest Coast. Natsilane had many talents, including woodcarving. Some people were jealous of Natsilane’s talents because they thought he would be chosen as the next chief. Natsilane could carve just about anything out of any type of material including rock and wood. His favorite material to work with was wood, and his wood carvings were of people, plants, and animals. Animals were his favorite to carve. He often went into the forest to observe animals so his carvings would be as realistic and lifelike as possible.
sources: Natsilane healed the sea lion’s flipper, and the sea lion safely returned Natsilane home, just as promised.

After he returned home, Natsilane began carving a large black fish from wood. After he finished the carving, Natsilane released it into the water, wishing that it would always be helpful and friendly to people just as the sea lion had been to him. To Natsilane’s surprise, his carved black fish came to life!

items.instructions: Jared is writing a report about how to choose a pet cat. Read the information he found on a reliable website. Then complete the table by putting check marks in the appropriate boxes to match each type of cat with its characteristics.
items.practiceHint: (Practice Hint: To place a check mark, select a blank box in the table. Select all appropriate boxes in each column. To erase a check mark you have placed, select it. To erase all check marks and start again, select the Clear All button.)

content.passage: Sphynx cats are an interesting breed. They look friendly and are quite curious and playful. They have huge ears and almost no hair, which makes the wrinkled skin on their heads and necks quite visible. Cornish rex cats also have an unusual appearance. Their heads are egg-shaped, and they have long legs and large ears. They have soft, wavy hair which they shed less than one might expect. These cats are extremely active, especially at their owners’ dinner time. Another breed of cat is the Devon rex. This is a fairly new breed and is good for busy families, since they are active, affectionate, and attentive, yet behave when their owners are away. These cats do not shed much of their wavy hair. They have very large ears and eyes.
sources: Wrinkled skin
Wavy hair
Large ears

items.instructions: Select two sentences from “An Amazing Day at the Aquarium” that show how the trip to the aquarium was educational for the narrator in some way. Drag and drop the answers.
items.practiceHint: (Practice Hint: Select the image below to view the answer choices. Then select-and-drag your responses into the box. To change just one answer, select-and-drag the answer out of the box. To change both answers and start again, select the Clear All button.)

content.passage: An Amazing Day at the Aquarium I couldn’t believe what I was hearing: our class was going on a field trip to the City Aquarium! I had always wanted to visit a giant aquarium. Ever since I was little, I was interested in all things aquatic. I wanted to be a marine biologist, and since I had watched a documentary about how life in our oceans is in danger, I was even more interested. When the day of the field trip finally arrived, we all climbed aboard buses to ride downtown. After 30 minutes, the bus pulled up to a building and parked. I was so excited to learn more about the marine life. Once inside the aquarium, I found it to be even more fascinating than I had ever imagined. The first exhibit we went to featured sea life from the reefs in the Caribbean Sea. There were turtles, eels, and many other creatures were amazing, and the fish were so colorful. Then, we were off to see the glowing jellyfish. There were thousands of them. Some were tiny and others were huge. I learned that they aren’t really fish. I also found out that I never wanted to get stung by one. There were other exhibits too. The reptile exhibit had alligators and giant snakes. I thought they were very interesting, especially their scaly skins. Then, we finally went to the mammal exhibit to see the whales, dolphins, sea otters, and sea lions.
sources: “I had always wanted to visit a giant aquarium.”
“The first exhibit we went to featured sea life from the reefs in the Caribbean Sea.”
“I learned that they aren’t really fish.”
“I learned so much more about marine life at the aquarium.”
“I also found out that I never wanted to get stung by one.”

items.instructions: Read the summary of “An Amazing Day at the Aquarium.” Then select the one sentence that needs to be added to make the summary complete.
items.practiceHint: (Practice Hint: When you select a sentence below, the sentence will become highlighted. To change your answer, select the Clear All button or select a different sentence.)

content.passage: An Amazing Day at the Aquarium I couldn’t believe what I was hearing: our class was going on a field trip to the City Aquarium! I had always wanted to visit a giant aquarium. Even when I was little, I was interested in all things aquatic. I had dreamed of being a marine biologist, and since I had watched a show on how the sea life in our oceans is in danger, I was even more interested. When the day of the field trip finally arrived, our class climbed aboard buses to ride downtown. After 30 minutes, the bus pulled up to a building and parked. I was so excited. I couldn’t wait to see the marine life. Once inside the aquarium, I found it was more fascinating than I had ever imagined. The first exhibit we went to featured sea life from the reefs in the Caribbean Sea. There were turtles, eels, and even parrot fish. The creatures were amazing, and the fish were vibrant! Then, we were off to see the glowing jellyfish. There were thousands of them. Some were tiny and others gigantic. I learned that they aren’t really fish. I also found out that I never wanted to get stung by one. There were other exhibits too. The reptile exhibit included alligators and giant snakes. I thought they were very interesting because of their scaly skins. Then, we finally went to see my favorite exhibit: the whales, dolphins, sea otters, and sea lions.
sources: There are many jellyfish to see at the aquarium.
The class visits many of the exhibits, and the narrator sees some interesting marine animals.
The narrator watches a show that describes why some of the wildlife found in the oceans are in danger.
The jellyfish at the aquarium are very pretty.

items.instructions: A student wrote a draft of a story. Arrange the events in the story in the sequence that makes the most sense.
items.practiceHint: Read all the events first. To move an event, use the Pointer tool to select-and-drag it to the correct position in the sequence.

sources: That is when a great idea came to me, so I immediately squeezed globs of different shades of green onto my tray.
After dinner, I went to my room, where my art notebook was open to a page with a few rough, black lines.
For the third night in a row, I sat down in front of the mostly blank piece of paper and searched my mind for creative ideas.
Feeling confident and excited, I dabbed my brush into the paint and, along the dark lines, started making broad strokes in the shape of a pine tree.
I leaned forward and put my head in my hands, and the green carpet caught my attention.

items.instructions: A student is writing a report about the papermaking process. Complete the table by putting a check mark next to each sentence to show whether the style of the sentence is formal or informal.
items.practiceHint: To place a check mark, use the Pointer tool to select an empty box in the table. To erase a check mark you have placed, select it. To erase all the check marks you have placed and start again, select the Clear All button.

sources: It is kind of wild that a thin, weak piece of paper comes from really, really big, strong trees.
To be made into paper, wood must be changed into a liquid substance called pulp.
Pulp is created by grinding wood chips or using chemicals to break wood into tiny pieces.
When pulp is spread out super thin, all the water gets kind of pressed out until you’ve got paper.

content.sentence: While I like playing the guitar for my friends, I like [either] taking lessons on Saturday mornings when all my friends are still sleeping [but also] practicing for a half hour every single day of the week.
